Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|WD=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|WD=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|WD=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|WD=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|WD=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|WD=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|WD=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|WD=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. designated March 18, 1997. === Access information === '''Address''' * Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} '''Transportation''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Banetsu West Line|Q807038|WD=Q807038|1=Ban'etsu West Line}} / {{ill|lt=Tadami Line|Q1054717|WD=Q1054717|1=Tadami Line}}, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu Station|Q4699462|WD=Q4699462|1=Aizu-Wakamatsu Station}} (about 11 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{УёџТ│есЃўсЃФсЃЌ}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine origin record (Kogaikuni Shrine) * {{Cite book|ja|editor=Meiji Jinja Shiryo Hensanjo|year=1912|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gosha Meiji Jinja Shiryo|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken G┼Їsha Meiji Jinja Shiry┼Ї}}|publisher=Meiji Jinja Shiryo Hensanjo|ref={{SfnRef|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|year=1993|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}} 7: Place names of Fukushima|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{SfnRef|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}|author=Katsuhiro Kiguchi|year=1984|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title=Nihon no Kamigami: Jinja to Seichi 12: Tohoku-Hokkaido|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022224|ref={{SfnRef|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}}}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=Aizu Fudoki|Q11381284|WD=Q11381284|1=Aizu Fudoki}} * {{ill|lt=Kokage Shrine|Q17228464|WD=Q17228464|1=Kokage Shrine}} - [[JA:УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ]] {{wikidata link|Q11625493}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Kogaikuni Shrine
{{Small|Kogaikuni Jinja}} | image = [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja keidai.JPG|270px]]
Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|WD=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|WD=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|WD=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|WD=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. File:Kogaikuni-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Kogaikuni-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kogaikuni-jinja shinsenjo.JPG|Shinsenjo File:Kogaikuni-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kaguraden === Subsidiary shrines === [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja keidaisha.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Precinct shrine}}]] * Gosha Inari ShrineSubsidiary-shrine list per shrine origin record. * Oniwatari Shrine * Gochureisha * Munakata Shrine and Takinao Shrine * Wareireisha === Festivals === * Tsukinamisai (1st and 15th of each month)Festival list per shrine origin record. * New Year's Day Festival (January 1) * Hatsuuma Festival (first "horse" day of February) * Oka Festival / Spring Grand Festival (April 19) * Summer purification and chinowa rite (June 30) * Grand Festival (August 1) * Mikoshi procession (August 2) * New Grain Thanksgiving Festival (November 23) * Year-end purification (December 30) === Cultural properties === === Aizuwakamatsu City designated natural monument === * Minehari-zakura - designated March 18, 1997. === Access information === '''Address''' * Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} '''Transportation''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Banetsu West Line|Q807038|WD=Q807038|1=Ban'etsu West Line}} / {{ill|lt=Tadami Line|Q1054717|WD=Q1054717|1=Tadami Line}}, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu Station|Q4699462|WD=Q4699462|1=Aizu-Wakamatsu Station}} (about 11 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{УёџТ│есЃўсЃФсЃЌ}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine origin record (Kogaikuni Shrine) * {{Cite book|ja|editor=Meiji Jinja Shiryo Hensanjo|year=1912|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gosha Meiji Jinja Shiryo|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken G┼Їsha Meiji Jinja Shiry┼Ї}}|publisher=Meiji Jinja Shiryo Hensanjo|ref={{SfnRef|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|year=1993|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}} 7: Place names of Fukushima|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{SfnRef|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}|author=Katsuhiro Kiguchi|year=1984|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title=Nihon no Kamigami: Jinja to Seichi 12: Tohoku-Hokkaido|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022224|ref={{SfnRef|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}}}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=Aizu Fudoki|Q11381284|WD=Q11381284|1=Aizu Fudoki}} * {{ill|lt=Kokage Shrine|Q17228464|WD=Q17228464|1=Kokage Shrine}} - [[JA:УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ]] {{wikidata link|Q11625493}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Kogaikuni Shrine
{{Small|Kogaikuni Jinja}} | image = [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja keidai.JPG|270px]]
Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|WD=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|WD=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|WD=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|WD=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. File:Kogaikuni-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Kogaikuni-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kogaikuni-jinja shinsenjo.JPG|Shinsenjo File:Kogaikuni-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kaguraden === Subsidiary shrines === [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja keidaisha.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Precinct shrine}}]] * Gosha Inari ShrineSubsidiary-shrine list per shrine origin record. * Oniwatari Shrine * Gochureisha * Munakata Shrine and Takinao Shrine * Wareireisha === Festivals === * Tsukinamisai (1st and 15th of each month)Festival list per shrine origin record. * New Year's Day Festival (January 1) * Hatsuuma Festival (first "horse" day of February) * Oka Festival / Spring Grand Festival (April 19) * Summer purification and chinowa rite (June 30) * Grand Festival (August 1) * Mikoshi procession (August 2) * New Grain Thanksgiving Festival (November 23) * Year-end purification (December 30) === Cultural properties === === Aizuwakamatsu City designated natural monument === * Minehari-zakura - designated March 18, 1997. === Access information === '''Address''' * Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} '''Transportation''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Banetsu West Line|Q807038|WD=Q807038|1=Ban'etsu West Line}} / {{ill|lt=Tadami Line|Q1054717|WD=Q1054717|1=Tadami Line}}, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu Station|Q4699462|WD=Q4699462|1=Aizu-Wakamatsu Station}} (about 11 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{УёџТ│есЃўсЃФсЃЌ}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine origin record (Kogaikuni Shrine) * {{Cite book|ja|editor=Meiji Jinja Shiryo Hensanjo|year=1912|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gosha Meiji Jinja Shiryo|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken G┼Їsha Meiji Jinja Shiry┼Ї}}|publisher=Meiji Jinja Shiryo Hensanjo|ref={{SfnRef|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|year=1993|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}} 7: Place names of Fukushima|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{SfnRef|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}|author=Katsuhiro Kiguchi|year=1984|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title=Nihon no Kamigami: Jinja to Seichi 12: Tohoku-Hokkaido|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022224|ref={{SfnRef|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}}}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=Aizu Fudoki|Q11381284|WD=Q11381284|1=Aizu Fudoki}} * {{ill|lt=Kokage Shrine|Q17228464|WD=Q17228464|1=Kokage Shrine}} - [[JA:УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ]] {{wikidata link|Q11625493}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Kogaikuni Shrine
{{Small|Kogaikuni Jinja}} | image = [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja keidai.JPG|270px]]
Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|WD=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|WD=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|WD=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|WD=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. File:Kogaikuni-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Kogaikuni-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kogaikuni-jinja shinsenjo.JPG|Shinsenjo File:Kogaikuni-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kaguraden === Subsidiary shrines === [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja keidaisha.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Precinct shrine}}]] * Gosha Inari ShrineSubsidiary-shrine list per shrine origin record. * Oniwatari Shrine * Gochureisha * Munakata Shrine and Takinao Shrine * Wareireisha === Festivals === * Tsukinamisai (1st and 15th of each month)Festival list per shrine origin record. * New Year's Day Festival (January 1) * Hatsuuma Festival (first "horse" day of February) * Oka Festival / Spring Grand Festival (April 19) * Summer purification and chinowa rite (June 30) * Grand Festival (August 1) * Mikoshi procession (August 2) * New Grain Thanksgiving Festival (November 23) * Year-end purification (December 30) === Cultural properties === === Aizuwakamatsu City designated natural monument === * Minehari-zakura - designated March 18, 1997. === Access information === '''Address''' * Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} '''Transportation''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Banetsu West Line|Q807038|WD=Q807038|1=Ban'etsu West Line}} / {{ill|lt=Tadami Line|Q1054717|WD=Q1054717|1=Tadami Line}}, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu Station|Q4699462|WD=Q4699462|1=Aizu-Wakamatsu Station}} (about 11 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{УёџТ│есЃўсЃФсЃЌ}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine origin record (Kogaikuni Shrine) * {{Cite book|ja|editor=Meiji Jinja Shiryo Hensanjo|year=1912|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gosha Meiji Jinja Shiryo|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken G┼Їsha Meiji Jinja Shiry┼Ї}}|publisher=Meiji Jinja Shiryo Hensanjo|ref={{SfnRef|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|year=1993|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}} 7: Place names of Fukushima|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{SfnRef|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}|author=Katsuhiro Kiguchi|year=1984|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title=Nihon no Kamigami: Jinja to Seichi 12: Tohoku-Hokkaido|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022224|ref={{SfnRef|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}}}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=Aizu Fudoki|Q11381284|WD=Q11381284|1=Aizu Fudoki}} * {{ill|lt=Kokage Shrine|Q17228464|WD=Q17228464|1=Kokage Shrine}} - [[JA:УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ]] {{wikidata link|Q11625493}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Kogaikuni Shrine
{{Small|Kogaikuni Jinja}} | image = [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja keidai.JPG|270px]]
Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|WD=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|WD=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|WD=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|WD=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|WD=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|WD=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|WD=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|WD=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|WD=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|WD=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|WD=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|WD=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. [[JA:УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ]] {{wikidata link|Q11625493}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Kogaikuni Shrine
{{Small|Kogaikuni Jinja}} | image = [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja keidai.JPG|270px]]
Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|WD=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|WD=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|WD=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|WD=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. File:Kogaikuni-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Kogaikuni-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden File:Kogaikuni-jinja shinsenjo.JPG|Shinsenjo File:Kogaikuni-jinja kaguraden.JPG|Kaguraden === Subsidiary shrines === [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja keidaisha.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Precinct shrine}}]] * Gosha Inari ShrineSubsidiary-shrine list per shrine origin record. * Oniwatari Shrine * Gochureisha * Munakata Shrine and Takinao Shrine * Wareireisha === Festivals === * Tsukinamisai (1st and 15th of each month)Festival list per shrine origin record. * New Year's Day Festival (January 1) * Hatsuuma Festival (first "horse" day of February) * Oka Festival / Spring Grand Festival (April 19) * Summer purification and chinowa rite (June 30) * Grand Festival (August 1) * Mikoshi procession (August 2) * New Grain Thanksgiving Festival (November 23) * Year-end purification (December 30) === Cultural properties === === Aizuwakamatsu City designated natural monument === * Minehari-zakura - designated March 18, 1997. === Access information === '''Address''' * Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} '''Transportation''' * Rail: {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Banetsu West Line|Q807038|WD=Q807038|1=Ban'etsu West Line}} / {{ill|lt=Tadami Line|Q1054717|WD=Q1054717|1=Tadami Line}}, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu Station|Q4699462|WD=Q4699462|1=Aizu-Wakamatsu Station}} (about 11 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{УёџТ│есЃўсЃФсЃЌ}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine origin record (Kogaikuni Shrine) * {{Cite book|ja|editor=Meiji Jinja Shiryo Hensanjo|year=1912|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gosha Meiji Jinja Shiryo|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken G┼Їsha Meiji Jinja Shiry┼Ї}}|publisher=Meiji Jinja Shiryo Hensanjo|ref={{SfnRef|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|year=1993|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}} 7: Place names of Fukushima|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{SfnRef|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}|author=Katsuhiro Kiguchi|year=1984|chapter=Kogaikuni Shrine|title=Nihon no Kamigami: Jinja to Seichi 12: Tohoku-Hokkaido|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022224|ref={{SfnRef|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}}}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=Aizu Fudoki|Q11381284|WD=Q11381284|1=Aizu Fudoki}} * {{ill|lt=Kokage Shrine|Q17228464|WD=Q17228464|1=Kokage Shrine}} - [[JA:УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ]] {{wikidata link|Q11625493}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Kogaikuni Shrine
{{Small|Kogaikuni Jinja}} | image = [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja keidai.JPG|270px]]
Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|WD=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|WD=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|WD=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|WD=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|WD=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|qid=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|qid=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|qid=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|qid=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|qid=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|qid=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|qid=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|qid=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|qid=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|qid=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|qid=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|qid=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|qid=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|qid=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|qid=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City. Precinct (Minehari-zakura on the left, haiden at rear right) | location = Kogaimachi 2-1, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|qid=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|qid=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{Coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kogaikuni Shrine}} | deity = {{Ublist|{{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}}|{{ill|qid=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|qid=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}}|{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}}}} | status = {{Ublist|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (small)|former {{ill|lt=prefectural shrine|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=kensha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}}} | founded = (trad.) Konin 2 (811) | architecture = Oji-zukuri | other_name = Kogaigu | annual_festival = {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|qid=Q2788|1=August 1}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|relief=1|label=Kogaikuni Shrine|position=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|width=200|float=center|border=none|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|37|30|17.0|N|139|56|19.7|E}}}} }} [[File:Kogaikuni-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Kogaikuni Shrine''' (сЂЊсЂїсЂёсЂЈсЂФсЂўсѓЊсЂўсѓЃ) is a Shinto shrine in Kogaimachi, {{ill|lt=Aizuwakamatsu|Q237699|qid=Q237699|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|qid=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}; under the modern ranking system, it was a {{ill|lt=kensha|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. Its formal name is '''Kogaikuni Jinja''' (УаХжцітюІуЦъуцЙ), and it is also called '''Kogaigu'''. Its crest is the "Aizu Mitsu-aoi."Shrine origin record. It is known as a shrine associated with sericulture. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities. * {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi-no-Okami|1=Ukemochi}} * {{ill|qid=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi|UNKNOWN|qid=Q10921330|lt=Wakumusubi-no-Okami|1=Wakumusubi}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|1=Amaterasu}} === History === === Foundation === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in 811 (Konin 2). In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmy┼Їch┼Ї}}, no other official shrine bears the name "Kogaikuni," making this name unique to this shrine. Scholars have argued that its foundation was related to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sericulture|1=UNKNOWN}}, and that it may have reflected a central-government policy to promote sericulture in Aizu rather than merely local custom.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} As background, the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|qid=Q1077029|1=Nihon K┼Їki}}'' entry for November 8, 796 records that women from several provinces were dispatched to Mutsu for two years to teach sericulture techniques.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕуЦъсђЁ№╝Ѕ|1984}} === Historical outline === In the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho'' compiled in 927 (Encho 5), the shrine is listed as "Kogaikuni Shrine" in Aizu District, {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|qid=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}}, and classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Of the Engishiki shrines in Aizu District, only Kogaikuni Shrine and {{ill|lt=Isasumi Shrine|Q3155136|qid=Q3155136|1=Isasumi Shrine}} are listed.{{Sfn|УџЋжцітЏйуЦъуцЙ№╝ѕт╣│тЄАуцЙ№╝Ѕ|1993}} Although it is not clearly recorded in the official national histories, shrine tradition says it was elevated to an official shrine in the Jowa era (834-848) by petition of Fujiwara no Fujimaro, then Azechi and Commander of the Chinju-fu, and that shrine buildings were first established in 1011 by local officials including Ishibe Shosho Michihide. The buildings were later lost to warfare and declined, but were restored by {{ill|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|Q837236|qid=Q837236|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}, first lord of the {{ill|lt=Aizu Domain|Q4389944|qid=Q4389944|1=Aizu Domain}}, who also confirmed an endowment of 20 koku. In 1743 the shrine rank was raised to Shoichii; after another fire in 1807, it was rebuilt in 1819 by the 8th lord, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Katataka|Q846200|qid=Q846200|1=Matsudaira Katataka}}. After the Meiji Restoration, in November 1876 (Meiji 9), it was listed as a prefectural shrine in the modern shrine ranking system.{{Sfn|ТўјТ▓╗уЦъуцЙУфїТќЎ|1912}} === Precinct === The main sanctuary is in Oji-zukuri (also called Kumano-zukuri). Other structures include a heiden, haiden, shinsenjo, and kaguraden. An old cherry tree standing before the haiden is said to be over 1,000 years old and 14 meters tall; it is especially called '''Minehari-zakura'''.Precinct information board. Shrine tradition says it was planted when the shrine buildings were established in 1011. It is designated a natural monument of Aizuwakamatsu City.
